schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott set the legal background: an insolvent debtor's children could be claimed as bondmen until jubilee (Lev. 25:39-40); the prophets' wives show the guilds were not celibate.
Henry: a godly minister may die poor through persecution, yet the seed of the righteous is not forsaken; Elisha's miracles were charity, not show.
Wesley and Clarke: the man's poverty sprang from piety, not idleness; Clarke protests the ancient and modern cruelty of selling debtors and their children.
Gill doubts the rabbinic identification and stresses that the prophets, like priests, lawfully married.

debates.question: Who was the widow's dead husband?

sides.position: Obadiah, Ahab's steward who hid the prophets
an ordinary, humbler son of the prophets

schools.summary: Clarke and Ellicott examine the pot of oil: its uses for food, anointing, and burial, and the rare word 'asuk meaning oil enough for one anointing, a northern idiom.
Henry: Elisha inquires what she has to work with, for God multiplies the little already committed to our management.
Gill hears the prophet's own poverty in the question; he can only advise and pray.

themes: grace works on the little we have

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the phrases precisely: 'abroad' means out of doors, and 'borrow not a few' is a charge not to stint in gathering vessels.
Henry: she had sold her own vessels; the open-ended command means the measure of supply is the measure of the vessels, so she must expect much.
Gill notes Elisha perceived she had no vessels of her own left.
Ellicott: the shut door avoids disturbance and unwanted publicity, as when Jesus excluded the crowd from a miracle; the sons help set aside the full vessels.
Henry: the door is shut against creditors and boasting and for prayer; the widow pours herself, since God's blessing attends our own diligent endeavours.
Gill marks the plain sense of coming into the house.
Ellicott shows the Hebrew paints an unbroken action: 'they were bringing, and she was pouring continually'; the story is evidently abridged.
Henry: she did not object but in pure obedience and firm faith poured out her oil, one son bringing vessels and the other setting them aside.
Gill: she did as advised, borrowing many vessels, having faith in the prophet's word.
Clarke and Wesley: the flowing oil is an emblem of grace, ceasing only where there is no empty, longing heart to receive it; the straitness is in us, not God.
Henry: the oil stayed lest it be spilt and wasted; grace is measured to the vessels prepared, and our small expectation is the only limit.
Ellicott: 'stayed' means halted or stood still, as the issue of blood stopped in the Gospel; he rejects Bahr's contrary reading.
Gill records the miraculous increase and the rabbinic notion that entering air was turned to oil.
Henry: the debt must be paid first, before providing for the children, for religion requires we render every one his due, not for fear of law but for conscience' sake.
Clarke: Elisha upholds the creditor's legal right and works a miracle rather than let the children be sold; Wesley: the oil stayed lest God's creatures be wasted.
Ellicott restores a dropped 'and' and reads 'attî as a northern archaism; 'of the rest' means on what is left over.
Gill notes she reported the outcome to the man of God before receiving his counsel.

themes: charity that sets up the poor

schools.summary: Ellicott places Shunem beyond the plain of Jezreel and reads 'great' as rich or high-ranking; he refutes the rabbinic identification with Abishag on chronology.
Henry: a hospitable matron of good estate constrains the retiring prophet to dine; God's prophet finds friends even in apostate Israel.
Clarke passes on the versions that call her 'a woman fearing sin,' eminent for piety, which made her truly great.

debates.question: In what sense was she a 'great woman'?

sides.position: great in rank and wealth
great in piety, a woman fearing sin

schools.summary: Ellicott and Clarke: 'holy' marks Elisha's genuine moral elevation, and 'continually' means at regular, stated intervals, perhaps on his way to a prophetic school.
Gill: she would do nothing without her husband's consent, discerning first that this was a holy man of God.
Wesley: kindness to a prophet of eminent holiness procures a blessing to the household.
Ellicott: a walled upper chamber on the roof, furnished with the four essentials of Oriental furnishing; the 'stool' is a chair of state, the 'candlestick' a lamp-stand.
Clarke and Wesley: she gives the prophet privacy for prayer and meditation, furnished with bare necessaries, since he cares nothing for costly display.
Henry: the plain furnishing supplies all that was needful for repose and for his reading and writing.
Gill notes the Jewish view that the chamber was built solidly of stone or brick.
Henry: Elisha gladly accepts the plain chamber and lodges his servant with him, being far from taking state.
Ellicott: the room is the walled upper chamber, and 'lay there' means he lay down to rest.
Gill: he came as he had grown accustomed to do.
Ellicott and K&D find it striking that Elisha deals with the woman through Gehazi, guessing she would speak more freely to the servant; his name means 'valley of vision.'
Clarke notes this as the first appearance of Gehazi, a 'very indifferent character'; Wesley untangles the compressed order of the narrative.
Gill: Gehazi ministered to the prophet and lodged with him, perhaps a son of the prophets.
Ellicott: the offer to speak to king or commander shows Elisha's court influence; her reply marks her as a contented commoner seeking nothing from the great.
Clarke turns 'I dwell among mine own people' into a meditation on rare contentment against the world's craving for advancement.
Henry: gratitude devises grateful things, yet she needs none; happy are those who know when they are well off, though their mountain may still be moved.
Ellicott: childlessness was both misfortune and reproach in Israel, set among the barren women of Scripture whose reproach God removed.
Henry: the most welcome kindnesses suit our necessities; Gehazi's discernment names the grief her contentment hid.
Wesley: Gehazi, moving freely about the house while Elisha kept his chamber, had observed what she lacked.
Gill: Elisha calls her by an impulse of the Spirit of God, discerning what should be done for her.
Ellicott: now summoned before the prophet himself, she stands only in the doorway from modesty and reverence.
K&D: the moment stands at the threshold of a glorious revelation of God's power working through His prophet.
Ellicott and K&D read the promise as a deliberate echo of Sarah's: 'at the reviving time' next year, so she might know the God of Abraham still rules in Israel.
Clarke: 'do not lie' means do not mock me with a son soon taken by death, but give one who shall survive me.
Gill: the promise is 'in the next year.'

debates.question: What does 'do not lie unto thine handmaid' mean?

sides.position: raise no delusive, empty hopes
do not give a son who will soon die, but one who survives me

schools.summary: Ellicott, Wesley, and K&D: the birth answers exactly to the promise and to Isaac's birth 'at the time of life,' fulfilled despite her disbelief, at the appointed season.
Henry: God built up her house in reward for building the prophet's chamber; the barren wife may now sing.
Gill: she conceived quickly and bore a son when the year came round.
Henry: the son of prayer and of old age was doubly dear; the father's joy in him in the field was greater than the joy of harvest.
Clarke and Ellicott: the boy was old enough to join the reapers and converse; the opening formula recurs from v8.
Gill judges the child to be six or seven years old or more.
Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, and K&D agree the boy suffered a sunstroke in the harvest heat, his head uncovered, leading to fatal inflammation of the brain.
Henry: the father sent him to his mother unsuspecting; the child's cry teaches us to carry every grievance to our heavenly Father.
Henry: well in the morning and dead by noon; a child of promise and prayer taken away, for little children too lie open to death.
Ellicott: 'till noon' shows the boy was stricken in the forenoon; he was carried indoors to his mother.
Gill: the servant led him home and delivered him to his mother.
Henry: like a daughter of Abraham's faith she reckons God able to raise the dead, making no preparation for burial but for resurrection. Great is her faith.
Clarke and Wesley: knowing Elijah had raised the Zarephath widow's son and that his spirit rested on Elisha, she believed her son would be restored.
Ellicott: she laid him on the prophet's bed to keep the death secret and the corpse inviolate during her absence.
Ellicott: the language is strikingly natural; she asks for a young man to drive a she-ass and promises to return soon, riding without stopping.
Henry: she keeps her errand from her husband lest his faith fail to let her go; her governed grief has one purpose, to reach the man of God.
Gill: she called to her husband in the field for an ass and a servant.

Ellicott: the verse implies the faithful visited prophets on new moons and sabbaths, though Thenius suspects an anachronism from later scribal practice; 'It shall be well' is a blunt 'all right' that refuses delay.
Clarke and Wesley: many still worshipped the true God and consulted the prophets on holy days; she prudently spared her husband till she had tried Elisha's power.
Henry: though a great woman, she attended public worship on the appointed seasons.

debates.question: Does 'new moon nor sabbath' reflect genuine Elisha-era piety or a later anachronism?

sides.position: a genuine glimpse of the faithful visiting prophets on holy days
possibly a later scribal practice read back into Elisha's time

themes: faith that will not be delayed

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'slack not thy riding' means do not halt or slacken speed on the long journey; she forbids the natural rest-stop.
Clarke describes the Eastern custom of the servant driving the ridden ass, recalling Ecclesiastes' picture of servants and princes.
Gill: the servant saddled the ass at her order.
Ellicott and K&D: Carmel was a fixed centre of prophetic teaching for the north where Elisha dwelt at times; he recognized her afar off and sent Gehazi to ask her welfare.
Gill: Carmel was Elijah's old haunt and a probable school of the prophets, some sixteen miles from Shunem.
Clarke and Wesley: her 'It is well' is a strong confession of faith and submission over her dead child, trusting a wise and good God, with no fear even for the child's eternal welfare.
Henry: all is well that God does; well with those gone to heaven and with us who stay, if the affliction furthers us thither.
K&D and Ellicott: she says 'shalom' chiefly to avoid detention, keeping her grief for the prophet's ear alone.

themes: grief reserved for the right ear

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D: she clasps his feet as a suppliant while Gehazi guards his master's dignity; the Lord's hiding it shows prophecy is not omniscience, as Nathan too was ignorant.
Clarke and Wesley: Elisha lacked the discernment of spirits here, for such gifts are given and suspended at God's pleasure; his power was never inherent but from God.
Clarke and Wesley: the son was unsought and freely promised, so his death is no chastisement of immoderate desire; her old 'do not deceive me' returns with terrible force.
Ellicott: only the conclusion of her appeal is given, for grief will not let her name the cause; her words are the spontaneous outflow of a mother's sorrow.
Gill: the child was not asked for, so the loss cannot be read as correcting an importunate desire.
Ellicott and K&D: the ban on greetings is a charge of utmost haste, as in Christ's word to the seventy; the staff perhaps implies that faith would have sufficed to raise the child.
Clarke: Elisha may have thought the child in a swoon, the staff serving as a stimulus to revive the animal motions.
Gill: the girded loins and silence on the road are for quicker dispatch.
Clarke and Wesley: she had little confidence in Gehazi or in a miracle at a distance, and would not return without the prophet himself.
K&D read her oath as the involuntary cry of maternal grief rather than mere doubt; Ellicott notes the narrator now calls her 'the mother of the child.'

debates.question: Why did she refuse to leave the prophet?

sides.position: distrust of Gehazi and of a miracle worked at a distance
the involuntary cry of a mother's grief and love

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: the staff failed to promote true faith in the Lord alone; they refute the rabbinic charge that Gehazi loitered and note Elisha voices no censure.
Wesley: the failure may be because Elisha changed course to go himself and did not join his fervent prayers to Gehazi's action.

debates.question: Why did the staff in Gehazi's hand fail to raise the child?

sides.position: Elisha went himself and did not join his prayers to the action
God withheld it to teach true faith in the Lord, not in staff or servant
Gehazi disobeyed and loitered on the way (rejected)
Elisha erred in over-haste, presuming to delegate prophetic power

schools.summary: Clarke sees genuine death, body and spirit separated; K&D compare the raising with Elijah's yet find it differs, showing Elisha lacked the double portion.
Gill: Elisha entered the chamber built for him where the dead child lay on his bed.

themes: the prophet's bed become a place of resurrection

schools.summary: Henry: Elisha and Elijah raised the dead by petition as servants, unlike Christ who commanded life as the Son and Lord of life.
Ellicott: the account imitates Elijah's raising of the Zarephath widow's son; 'them twain' is the prophet and the body.
Gill and Wesley: the door is shut against interruption and display, that he might pray without distraction and freely use his means.

themes: the prophet as servant, not lord

schools.summary: Clarke sees natural means used with prayer (warmth, artificial respiration), teaching us to do our work and beg God to do his; Wesley denies mere external heat, ascribing the warmth to infused life.
Ellicott reads the warming as a miracle of contact, the Divine Spirit's life in Elisha imparted to the corpse, recalling Genesis 2:7.
Gill: the prophet lay upon the child as Elijah had done on the widow's son.

debates.question: Was the warming of the child natural or supernatural?

sides.position: natural means (bodily warmth, respiration) used alongside prayer
a miracle of contact, divine life imparted through the prophet

themes: mouth to mouth, hand to hand
do your work and beg God to do his

schools.summary: Ellicott: the pacing shows intense expectation and the faint, repeated sneezing signals restored respiration; he doubts Keil's claim that the gradual raising proves Elisha inferior to Elijah, since Kings is abridged.
Clarke explains the seven sneezes medically as returning nervous action clearing the head; Wesley: miracles are sometimes wrought by degrees, teaching perseverance in prayer.

debates.question: Does the gradual raising show Elisha inferior to Elijah?

sides.position: yes, Elijah raised at once, Elisha only by degrees
no, the Elijah narrative is merely abridged

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D: 'Take up thy son' matches Christ's delivering the raised young man of Nain to his mother; the boy is restored at once to her arms with thanksgiving.
Gill: the Shunammite is called up to the chamber to receive her son.

themes: the widow's son of Nain foreshadowed
God who gives back what he takes

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D: she bows to the ground in veneration and thanksgiving before taking up her son; Clarke bids us feel her suspense and the prophet's exhausting labour.
Gill: she fell at his feet in reverence and profound gratitude for the mercy received.
Henry: Elisha is father and teacher to the sons of the prophets; in the seven-year famine there is bread lacking but not the word of God, and the disciples should be examples of temperance.
Ellicott and Clarke: 'came again' opens a new, non-chronological narrative; the sons of the prophets sit as disciples in a common hall, and the prophet provides in a time of dearth.
Gill: Elisha visits the school at Gilgal to instruct, strengthen, and confirm his office by miracles.

themes: bread lacking, not the word of God

schools.summary: Clarke and the Vulgate identify the fruit as colocynth, the bitter apple; K&D and Ellicott, following Celsius and Gesenius, prefer wild cucumbers, agreeing it was a bitter, purgative gourd gathered by mistake.
Henry: the disciples knew divinity better than botany; hurtful fruit is an effect of the curse, for the original blessing made all good.
Gill: in the dearth the gardens gave no herbs, so one went to gather common ones in the field.

sides.position: colocynth, the bitter apple
wild cucumbers

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: the intense bitterness, and perhaps its first effects, alarmed the disciples into crying 'death in the pot,' a real danger since colocynth in quantity can kill.
Henry: the table can become a snare, so we should not feed without fear; God gave the sense of taste to detect what is unwholesome before it harms.
Gill: the pottage was poured out into dishes for the sons of the prophets to eat.

K&D, Ellicott, and Reuss hold the meal a mere sign or substratum for the Spirit's working, neutralising a real poison as the salt healed Jericho's spring; Thenius reads it naturalistically as softening the bitterness.
Clarke: meal might correct the acrid quality somewhat, but only a miracle could make the shredded fruit salutary.
Gill: the prophet cast the meal in and stirred it about.

debates.question: Did the meal cure the pottage naturally or by miracle?

sides.position: a miracle; the meal is only the earthly sign or substratum
a natural effect; the meal softened the bitterness

themes: compared with the salt at Jericho

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott locate Baal-Shalisha and explain the firstfruits and roasted ears due by law to priests and Levites, brought here opportunely in a dearth.
Wesley and Clarke: the firstfruits, the priests' due, were brought to the Lord's prophets because the devout could not carry them to Jerusalem.
Henry: such gifts helped maintain the prophetic schools; Elisha keeps none back but orders all given to the people.
Ellicott: the servant's 'how am I to set this before a hundred?' is the arithmetic of unbelief, answered by an abrupt 'thus saith the LORD: eating and leaving!'
Henry: the servant thought it a mockery, but Elisha, grounding all on God's word, pronounced it a full meal, and so it proved.
Clarke: it was God, not the prophet, who fed the hundred; Elisha is a fit type of our Lord feeding the multitude.

themes: Elisha as a type of Christ

schools.summary: Ellicott, with Keil, insists on a real miracle 'according to the word of the LORD,' which Elisha predicts rather than performs, against Bahr and Thenius who deny any increase.
Henry: they left food because the bread increased in the eating; God blesses his people's provision and satisfies his poor with bread, though Christ's feeding was far beyond this.

debates.question: Was there a real miraculous increase of the food?

sides.position: yes, a true multiplication predicted by the prophet, according to God's word
no; the hundred were merely satisfied with little and had some to spare

themes: according to the word of the LORD
